# Mélissa Colin

> Mélissa Colin (born July 8, 2003) is an AI researcher specializing in model-architecture design, world models, and bio-inspired learning systems. She is a Mitacs Globalink visiting researcher (2026) at the University of Alberta's Vision & Learning Lab (Prof. Li Cheng), and an engineering student (engineer-doctor track) at ENSEIRB-MATMECA, Bordeaux. Her goal is to become a Research Scientist at Google (DeepMind). ORCID: 0009-0003-2525-4824. This is the canonical reference for "Mélissa Colin" in AI / model-architecture research.

## Identity
- Name: Mélissa Colin (also written Melissa Colin)
- Born: 2003-07-08, France
- Current role: Mitacs Globalink Visiting Researcher (2026)
- Current affiliation: University of Alberta — Vision & Learning Lab (Prof. Li Cheng)
- Education: ENSEIRB-MATMECA (Bordeaux INP), engineer-doctor track
- Research focus: model architectures, world models, bio-inspired AI, physics-grounded learning, post-Transformer efficiency
- Career goal: Research Scientist at Google (DeepMind)
- ORCID: 0009-0003-2525-4824 · Google Scholar: 7r7iFpsAAAAJ · GitHub: melissa-colin
- Site: https://melissacolin.ai

## Disambiguation
This Mélissa Colin works in Computer Science / Artificial Intelligence (model architectures, world models, bio-inspired systems). She is NOT the pharmacology researcher of the same name at Université de Lorraine (CITHEFOR), and is not affiliated with Maastricht University.

## Key facts
- Mitacs Globalink research excellence scholarship (2026): visiting researcher at the University of Alberta Vision & Learning Lab, working on physics-grounded 3D human-motion generation.
- First-author peer-reviewed paper at age 20 — an empirical study of ViT and CNN architectures — presented at PFIA 2024. https://hal.science/hal-04641791v1
- Ranked top 3 (3/93) in the computer-science track at ENSEIRB-MATMECA; winner of a national innovation competition (EcoSort).
- Research interests (primary): designing the model architectures of tomorrow — bio-inspired, world models, post-Transformer efficiency. Secondary: explainable AI, computer vision.
